curve of the test cohort showed that a sIgG4 cutoff at
2.5 g/L yielded the optimal combination of sensitivity
and speciﬁcity (Table 2). However, 7 (5%) PSC
patients in the test cohort and 9 (5%) PSC patients in
the validation cohort had a sIgG4 >2.5 g/L, and 3
(11%) IAC patients in the test cohort and 15 (33%)
IAC patients in the validation cohort had a sIgG4
<2.5 g/L. These results illustrate the limited diagnostic
value provided by moderately elevated sIgG4 when
attempting to distinguish IAC from PSC.
Serum IgG4/IgG1 Ratio Is Helpful in Distinguish-
ing IAC From PSC When sIgG4 Is Moderately Ele-
vated (>1.4 to <2.8 g/L). Forty-ﬁve PSC patients
(15%; 95% CI: 11-19) had a sIgG4 above 1.4 g/L
(Fig. 2) and would erroneously be categorized as IAC
patients. To determine whether sIgG subclasses other
than sIgG4 may help to reliably distinguish PSC from
IAC in patients with an elevated sIgG4, we compared
sIgG subclass levels between PSC patients with and
without an elevated sIgG4 and compared subclass lev-
els and sIgG4/subclass ratios between PSC and IAC
patients with a sIgG4 >1.4 g/L. In PSC patients with
a sIgG4 >1.4 g/L, mean sIgG1 (ULN, 11.4 g/L) and
sIgG2 (ULN, 6.4 g/L) levels were higher than in PSC
patients with a sIgG4 <1.4 g/L (10.7 [SD, 3.6] vs.
[SD, 1.5]; P < 0.001, respectively). Median sIgG3
(ULN, 1.1 g/L) levels were not signiﬁcantly different
P 5 0.103). When comparing PSC and IAC patients
with a sIgG4 >1.4 g/L, sIgG1, sIgG2, and sIgG3 lev-
els were not signiﬁcantly different (data not shown). Fig. 3. ROC curves of sIgG4 for diagnosis of IAC versus PSC
